one_line_takeaway: Injecting even one vulnerable example into an RACG knowledge base can make ~48% of an LLM's generated code vulnerable while leaving functionality untouched -- a stealthy, cheap poisoning threat that is worst with dense retrievers and code-specialized LLMs.
key_finding: KB poisoning is a strong, low-cost threat that raises generated-code vulnerability while leaving functionality essentially unchanged. In Scenario I with JINA, a SINGLE poisoned example lifts CodeLlama's VR from 0.29 to 0.48 (the headline ~48%) and GPT-4o's from 0.26 to 0.42; further examples plateau (CodeLlama reaches 0.53 at 9 examples). Code-specialized LLMs are more susceptible than general ones (CodeLlama > DS-Coder > GPT-4o/Llama-3 under JINA). The dense retriever (JINA) is far more dangerous than sparse BM25 because it retrieves more relevant -- hence more poisoned -- examples (VRRC 0.41 vs 0.06 at 5 poisons; retriever MRR 0.85 vs 0.20). Scenario II (blind poisoning) is much harder: matching Scenario I's VRRC of ~0.42 requires a 100% poisoning proportion, yet even 20% injection still makes ~36-37% of CodeLlama output vulnerable. Secondary factors: more shots (one->three) increase VR (+6.5% aggregate under JINA-I, 0.46->0.49) while slightly improving similarity; C++ shows the highest VR and Java/Python the lowest; example-query similarity above 60% sharply increases VR (Scenario I aggregate 0.35 at [60,80) -> 0.55 at [80,100]); and CWE-352 (CSRF) is the most reliably reproduced weakness (~0.78-0.79) while CWE-434 (unrestricted file upload) is the least (0.26-0.36). Crucially, similarity-to-ground-truth barely moves with poisoning (e.g., DS-Coder JINA 0.76->0.78), so the attack is stealthy.
core_contribution: The first comprehensive empirical study of knowledge base (KB) poisoning as a security threat to Retrieval-Augmented Code Generation (RACG). Rather than proposing a new attack algorithm, the paper's own contribution is a systematic measurement of how vulnerable code examples injected into an RACG knowledge base propagate into LLM-generated code. It formalizes the poisoning attack as an optimization objective (maximize the fraction of queries whose generated code is judged vulnerable, over the constructed vulnerable-example set V), defines two realistic attacker scenarios (exposed vs. hidden programming intent), builds a purpose-made dataset with paired secure/vulnerable versions per query, and measures vulnerability propagation across 16 sub-scenarios (2 scenarios x 4 LLMs x 2 retrievers). It also contributes an LLM-as-judge extraction-detection pipeline for scoring generated-code security and a set of mitigation recommendations. The paper explicitly distinguishes its target (compromising the SECURITY of generated code while preserving functionality) from prior RAG-poisoning work such as PoisonedRAG, which targets factual/functional correctness.
threat_model: Attacker goal: inject a set of m vulnerable code examples V into the RACG knowledge base K so that the retriever surfaces them as in-context examples and the LLM reproduces the vulnerabilities in generated code, without degrading functionality (so developers do not discard the output). Attacker capability: can write to the public sources (e.g., GitHub) from which the KB is collected -- a realistic, previously-demonstrated assumption (paper cites Carlini et al. on practical web-scale poisoning). The attacker canNOT access or query the deployed RACG retriever's parameters, so an external 'poisoning retriever' (TE3 / text-embedding-3-large) is used offline to select/construct V. Two scenarios: (I) Exposed Programming Intent -- attacker knows/predicts developer queries and injects vulnerable code semantically matched to each query; (II) Hidden Programming Intent -- attacker cannot see queries and instead poisons blindly by clustering the KB (K-means, elbow method), selecting centroid-proximate representatives per cluster at a poisoning proportion p, and injecting the most-similar vulnerable example to each. Poisoning rates studied range from a single example (0.008% of the 12,053-item KB) up to a 100% proportion.
limitations: (1) The security label is produced by an LLM-as-judge whose validated accuracy is only ~77-81% (manual accuracy 0.72-0.84 across languages), so absolute VR numbers carry meaningful measurement error, though relative trends are more robust. (2) Queries for functions lacking comments were LLM-generated (DeepSeek-V2.5); manual review found only ~86% accurately reflect functionality, a source of noise the authors acknowledge. (3) Coverage is limited to four languages (C, C++, Java, Python -- ~42.7% of Q1-2024 GitHub PR activity) and does not include e.g. Go. (4) The study is a measurement/threat characterization: mitigations (alter retrieval to pick the 2nd-most-similar or randomize among top candidates; CWE-specific KB screening; concealing intent) are proposed and argued but NOT implemented or evaluated. (5) Functionality is proxied only by CrystalBLEU similarity to a reference, not by executing tests -- the authors note generated standalone functions are hard to compile, which also motivated using an LLM judge instead of static analysis. (6) The attacker is assumed unable to access the deployed retriever; a stronger white-box retriever attacker is out of scope.

datasets_used: ReposVul (selected base dataset; satisfied all 4 selection criteria)
Final constructed dataset: 12,053 functions, 236 distinct CWEs, across C (6,956 functions / 139 CWEs), C++ (510 / 62), Java (2,810 / 115), Python (1,777 / 110)
12 candidate datasets screened: Big-Vul, CVEFixes, D2A, DiverseVul, ESC, Juliet, NVD, ReposVul, Reveal, SARD, Smartbugs Wild, SolidiFi
MITRE 2024 CWE Top-25 used to group vulnerability-type results
CodeSearchNet cited as an example of prior (non-vulnerable) code-generation datasets, not used for the experiments
quantitative_results: Scenario I, JINA, CodeLlama VR: 0.29 (0 poison) -> 0.48 (1) -> 0.53 (9)
Scenario I, JINA, GPT-4o VR: 0.26 -> 0.42 with a single poisoned example
Scenario I, BM25, GPT-4o VR: 0.27 -> 0.33 across 0->9 poisons (weaker effect than JINA)
VRRC (fraction of retrieved code that is vulnerable) at 5 poisons: JINA 0.41 vs BM25 0.06
Scenario II, 20% (0.2) poisoning proportion: CodeLlama VR ~0.36 (JINA) / ~0.35 (BM25); text reports ~37%/35%
Scenario II needs 100% poisoning proportion to reach the VRRC (~0.42) that Scenario I reaches with 7 injected examples (~0.075% poison rate)
Few-shot one->three shots, aggregate VR: JINA-I 0.46->0.49 (+6.5%); BM25-I 0.33->0.34 (+3.0%)
Functionality (CrystalBLEU similarity) nearly flat under poisoning, e.g. DS-Coder JINA 0.76->0.78 over 0->9 poisons
Language-averaged VR (JINA-I): C++ 0.47, C 0.45, Java 0.46, Python 0.45; C++ generally highest across sub-scenarios (BM25-II avg C++ 0.44)
Example-query similarity vs VR (Scenario I aggregate): [0,20) 0.28-ish region rising to [60,80) 0.35 -> [80,100] 0.55; CodeLlama peaks at 0.57 in [80,100]
CWE-type VR (MITRE Top-10): CWE-352 CSRF highest at 0.79 (Scen I) / 0.78 (Scen II); CWE-434 lowest at 0.36 (Scen I) / 0.26 (Scen II)
LLM-judge validation: manual accuracy 0.76 (C), 0.72 (C++), 0.79 (Java), 0.84 (Python); automated F1 ~0.78-0.81; overall accuracy cited as 77-81%
Retriever effectiveness: JINA MRR 0.85, SR@1 0.79, SR@5 0.91, SR@10 0.93 vs BM25 MRR 0.20, SR@1 0.14, SR@5 0.19, SR@10 0.24
Generated-query quality: manual review found ~86% of LLM-generated queries accurately reflect function functionality
baselines_compared: Unpoisoned knowledge base (poisoning number 0 / proportion 0) as the security baseline for each LLM+retriever
Four LLMs compared: GPT-4o, Llama-3-8B, CodeLlama-13B, DeepSeek-Coder-V2-16B (general vs code-specialized)
Two retrievers compared: JINA (dense) vs BM25 (sparse)
Two attacker scenarios compared: Scenario I (exposed intent) vs Scenario II (hidden intent)
Conceptual contrast with PoisonedRAG / HijackRAG (RAG poisoning targeting factual/functional correctness) rather than an implemented numeric baseline
